I already introduced him. You know he is. You know why he's here. So launch expert of the world and did a really cool job with the certification program, which we haven't talked about much yet, but we will. So I leave you and Jeff's capable hands for a little while. And then I'll be back and we'll do some Q&A. So this is the quick version of Product Launch Formula. But first, before I get into that, basically Product Launch Formula is this thing I created. Wow, all of a sudden that sound just blew out at me. It's basically this method of selling that I created. It's to launch a product or relaunch a product. It could be launch a business or relaunch a business. This is an online thing. This is what it's basically, it's internet marketing. I mean, it can be used for an offline business. It could be used for an offline service. But it's primarily selling online. Works best for information products, which also includes seminars, membership sites, continuity, anything like that. It also works for e-commerce, for widgets. But the perfect fit is like the fit where you just drop it in and get instant money is if you have an information marketing business, especially if you already have a list, then it's just like instant money. So one other thing is, I'm not a professional speaker. I've certainly spoken plenty of times. I'm plenty comfortable up here. But this is something I completely made up this presentation for you folks, because I've never talked about this. I've never revealed this stuff at all. I have a Platinum Group, and I revealed a small portion of it to them on a call once. But otherwise, no one else has ever heard this story. This is a presentation. I have literally put this PowerPoint together over the last few days for you. So some of these slides, they're going to be as big a surprise to me as they are to you as they come out. And some of them, I hate having tons of text on a slide, but some of them, I had to do that because I need to remember what I'm going to tell you. This PowerPoint is for me to remember what to say. I can go on and on about this stuff, but that's what it's about. OK. I forgot to check what time it was. We're just going to say I got 59 minutes from now. OK. So in the last 58 hours, one of the things with these launches is I don't just teach them. I'm actually pretty good at them. And that's sort of self-deprecating. I'm really, really good at them. And so in the last 58 hours, I actually just did a product launch of my own. And closed at midnight Pacific. It closed at 3 o'clock our time. I got up. I set an alarm. Got up at like 3.34 and turned off the shopping car because that's when we said it was going to end. We gave people extra 34 minutes. And we did $1,534,758 in that 58. Thank you. Now, that's by far not my best 58 hours. But by the nature of this launch, this was sort of a relaunch of already existing products. So like last June, when we launched this product, we did $3.5 million in about the same amount of time. I've done crazy stuff like I had an hour, a single hour, 60 minutes where I did $1.1 million in sales. I had a second, one second where I did over $12,000 in sales. It's pretty fun because in your shopping cart, you can actually go back and look in the logs and see exactly what you did. And I picked out that one second where we got like six or seven orders every day, what it was, all in that very second. It was sort of crazy. And basically what it is is you're priming the pump. You're getting people very, very excited for when you're ready to roll out that product and launch it. So enough about me. This is a typical product launch formula owner. This is Dr. Andrew Jones, who's a veterinarian up in Nelson, British Columbia. He had an ebook. So it was just a PDF he was selling. PDF was selling it sort of on ClickBank, which is sort of lowest common denominator in terms of selling stuff online, in terms of the technology to get you there. And this $27 ebook was doing about, there you go. It was on Pet Care. It's about taking care of your cats and dogs. He was doing about $500 to $1,000 a month. This was just selling an ebook online. It was more a $500,000 for someone who's working for a living. That's a nice car, right? But he wanted the life-changing thing. He really, he was done with the whole being a vet thing. And so this is what he did. This is his business beforehand, doing that $500 to $1,000 a month. Then he got product launch formula. He put together a launch. He did a $60,000 launch. So he had this small list. He was selling stuff to him basically month after month. And then he pulled back and he did a launch. What he did was he took his $27 ebook. He turned it into a home study. He made it $300. Launched it. Went through the standard product launch. The sequences sold 200 of them at $300 plus created a continuity program off the back end. Did $60,000. If you do the math, that's like five years of sales in a week. And so this stuff works. And it works in all kinds of different niches. He not only got the immediate cash. He got this continuity income. He doubled the size of his list just through word of mouth. He was part of this as you put out a series of prelaunch content, what we call things that get people excited leading into it. And if you do a good enough job, people will start to pass that around virally. And you start to build your list that way. He attracted joint venture partners. He didn't have any of those before. His interaction with the list is a really big thing we do. It's a lot of interaction. Dan was talking earlier today about staying in touch with your client. And one of the things you literally have in a launch, in my recent launch, I had thousands of touches with my clients. Thousands of people leaving comments about my videos. And that's basically how I identify what the objections are. This is how anyone doing one of these launches, you identify your objections and what your offer should be in this prelaunch content. And of course, once you figure out what people want, you make that and try to sell it to them. That's the proverbial shooting fish in a barrel. So what markets, this is typically, everyone always wants to know, does that work in my business? The answer is unless you're in an emergency, like a locksmith, you're getting called in the middle of the night, or Dan mentioned the bail bondsman, probably not a good fit for product launch. If you're selling sand, a pure commodity, not a good match for product launch. Otherwise, yes, it's a good match. It's a bit of a hobby of mine, keeping track of all the crazy, different markets that people do these launches in. I can't remember which ones I have down here, but the last one is Trissach. So that's horse ballet. You all know what horse ballet is, right? I didn't. And one time I was on a stage, just after this woman did a $160,000 launch in Trissach. And I had no freaking clue what it was. So then I went and I googled it. Actually, I went to Wikipedia. Is that a verb? Wikipedia? So anyway, I went to Wikipedia, figured out it was the horse ballet. So then I got up on stage. I was so excited that she did this $160,000. This is a big room. There's 350, 400 people in this room. And I started talking about this woman, how she did this launch in the dressage market. And I was just all excited about this dress. And someone over on the side said, job job. That's Trissach. Like, hey, got me, got me. So anyways, it works in all kinds of different markets. And it's because it relies on basic human psychology, in mental triggers. And it gets people excited. The launch sequence gives you the time to build in reciprocity, to build in authority, massive authority. I put out these videos of me recently with this launch of me interviewing people for case studies. Just builds massive authority, builds commitment consistency. You can build actually community. It's a really powerful trigger. I mean, there's clearly a strong community here. I mean, it's just otherwise. Why else would you put up with the abuse that Dan heaps on you guys? I mean, it's like, and believe me, I'm one of you. I'm part of this community because I'm sitting out there and I've been looking at his stuff for a decade. But communities are like, these are all things that you build in your launch sequence that gets people focused on that launch day. And that's how you do $12,000 in a single second. OK, so what types of products does it work for? Just all kinds of different products. Physical products, marching band accessories, used marching band accessories. I mean, that's a pretty obscure one. Holstein cow embryos. But like I said, it's the most perfect fit for information products. It's been used really good for coaching, selling coaching, high-end coaching, platinum groups. Any type of live event, tele-seminars, webinars, online services, offline services, consulting, just goes on and on. Very, very adaptable. OK, so yeah, it's been pretty impactful. Basically, my clients, I have $200 million up here. I'm pretty sure it's at least $300 million, but I haven't actually gone and documented. I actually got a little lazy once I did $200 million and I stopped documenting it. So I got to go back and do it. And of course, I only hear from a small fraction of my Proclinx Formula owners that actually do launches by they're telling me about their great success. But I know it's over $200 million in launches. OK, so now I just want to, that's sort of the big rah-rah show, how awesome it is. Now, just want to give you a little bit about how it works. And then we're going to go into how I took this and sort of went meta on it and created the quote, unquote opportunity. Of course, it was not sold as an opportunity officially. But anyways, so I have this thing I call a sideways sales letter. And so how many of you have, say, ever been to an online sales letter? Anyone here seen an online sales letter? OK, so obviously we all have. And so what happens when you go to an online sales letter? It's like a lot of times when you land on it, you don't know you're going to land on that sales letter. Someone snuck a link to you via email and they said, this is the most awesome thing I've ever seen and click on it or whatever. Somehow you just land, you click on an ad, you land on that sales letter. So when that page first loads, there's that scroll bar off on the side there, over there, that little blue thing. That scroll bar, when a page first loads, it's like really big and then it gets smaller and smaller and smaller and smaller and smaller and smaller as well. As that page loads, as that 50 page sales letter loads. And we can do 50 page sales letters, because it doesn't cost us anything to print them. So we can do those nice, long sales letters. The reality is that when most people hit those sales letters, they take that scroll bar and scroll it right down to the bottom and they check out your prices. And they don't read the sales letter, right? Either that or they just click away. The sales letters, I mean, I love sales letters. I've written sales letters. I've written sales letters that have done many millions of dollars. I don't know if I can say tens of millions, but multiple millions. I like reading them. I bet you a lot of you guys like reading them too. I mean, they're like, it's a hobby, right? It's cool, because we're into it. But most people aren't reading them. And especially online, they're not terribly effective in terms of closing. I still use them. They're great, but they're not the most effective way to close. So I do this thing, what I call conceptually. This is one of the best teaching and selling tools I came up with, this idea of the sideways sales letter. Sort of a big idea. Instead of being 50 pages or 30 pages or 20 pages long, I said, oh, what if I turn it sideways? And I'd basically tell the same story and do the same thing you would do with a sales letter. And I'd tell it over, say, 10 days or 12 days. So what does a sales letter start with? There you go. It starts with a headline. And then typically, what do you guys like to do after a headline? Subhead. Double readership. Double readership path. OK. Typically, I'll go into a story pretty quickly after the headline. I'll try to pull them in with a story. And then usually the story will start to evolve into an opportunity, how to make your life better. And this is the opportunity I'm using in a bigger term, but an opportunity, how to make your life better, how to get thinner, how to get in better shape, how to get to the level of your life, how to make more money, how to play guitar better, how to play golf better. There's usually some opportunity for them to change their lives. So typically the way I'll do a sales letter, headline, story, or at least set the hook for a story, then an opportunity, then usually build out the story. And then eventually you get down there. You'll talk about bullet points, benefits. Get down further, and you start to develop the offer. Give them the offer. Further down, maybe some risk reversal, presentation of price. I mean, typical sales letter. Now if we took that and we said, instead of doing this over 50 pages, let's do it over like 10 days, or 12 days, or 14 days, or five days, or six days, and do it with three pieces, or maybe four pieces of content. Or they feel like content, but they're people who are really being sold. And you're leading them generally. There's no mention of the sale until maybe the third piece of the content. So the first piece, you're setting the hook for a story. You're starting to talk about the opportunity, getting people really excited. You're not selling them. They're like, well, this guy's not selling me anything. This is awesome. And their BS detector, which as soon as they hit that sales letter, their BS detector goes, wha, off into the red zone. But when they hit that first video, and you're not selling them, you're just telling them about this cool opportunity and actually giving them content, teaching them. You're really teaching them about the opportunity. You're not teaching them how to do it. You're teaching them about it. And all of a sudden, that BS detector starts to relax, and they start to love you. And they feel great about you. And then you give them the second piece. And now you take that opportunity, and you show how that opportunity, how people have applied that opportunity to their lives. Maybe people just like them have applied it to their lives. And now they just really can't figure out what the hell's going on. And then the third piece, now you basically give them more details about that opportunity. Actually give them, teach them more about what they need to do. And then usually about the 2 thirds of the way through that video, you start to mention, hey, I'm just really excited that so many of you have been able to check out my videos, and that I've been able to help. So many of you play guitar better. And I know so many of you guys are starting to play at F-Cord with some real authority. It's been really awesome sharing this stuff with you. And I want to tell you what I'm really excited about is I'm actually going to be creating this online coaching program for people that want to take to the next step and really start to put it together into songs. And I'm going to be telling you about that in my next video. And so that's sort of the little switch, the little switch that, hey, you're going to be sold something. And then you come back with the sales letter or the sales video, very, very effective. That's the sideways sales letter. Here's sort of the timeline. You start your pre-launch, you have pre-launch content. Here's some of the triggers we'll often hit. This isn't set in stone. We'll move these around to pan the exact offer, the pre-launch story. There's often a story arc that goes over the whole thing. It's almost like a novel where the excitement and the tension just builds up over time until you get to where you allow them to buy from you. And so these are just some of the triggers we'll often hit. We'll often start with authority. I mean, it's how I started this presentation. I got up here and I told you I made a million and a half bucks in the last 58 hours. That's a really effective way to start your presentations is with authority. Then you might move into reciprocity. So now you're giving them stuff. The reciprocity is usually you're giving them content, you're teaching them. Often you're just teaching them what they need to know, what they need to do, not how to do it. But you're teaching them. And so that builds this reciprocity. Then your community and social proof throughout the entire launch, people start, they can see other people getting excited. Maybe you're putting up videos and people are making comments they can see. I mean, when someone gets to one of my videos and they can see, there's like 1,100 comments people have made. And like 1,000 of them are, Jeff, you're awesome. I can't believe you're giving this away for free. That's mass social proof. It's real social proof. Massive. Then you also build it to community. Then as you get deeper into the launch, you start to build anticipation. Usually it's best if you have some scarcity involved. With this launch, that thing was open for 58 hours. They had 58 hours to buy from me. After about the first 24 hours, I told them, OK, now we're going to be closing tomorrow at midnight. And it spikes sales. Nothing closes sales like a hard deadline. So that's a typical launch sequence. So I had this great brand, or I have this great brand, product launch formula. Just really, really, really powerful brand. First started publishing it in 2005. And it really has changed the way stuff is sold online. Literally. Changed the way stuff is sold online. In all kinds of different markets and niches. People are making a lot. The people that learn guitar and niche, they used to be making a couple thousand bucks a month. Then they started doing these product launches. And they were making $5,000 or $6,000 for the launch. Now they're doing like $70,000 or $80,000 for the launch because they've really built up their market. So it's been very, very effective. So I've been teaching this since 2005. And it's been constantly updated. I open it up, let people register. Basically I run it as a coaching program. And I do this once or twice a year, basically. To my knowledge, and I have pretty deep knowledge about the internet marketing world, it's the most successful product ever teaching people how to do internet marketing that was sold online. Now I hear about some of these early days doing the malls and that kind of stuff. And it's primarily sold offline. It's online things sold off. Those guys probably got me beat. But in terms of my world, which is teaching internet marketing and teaching it, selling it online, teaching it online, I don't know anything. Anyone that's done this with one product, basically I've done about $17 million in sales. There's no end in sight to the awesome sauce. It just keeps on rolling along. The launches get bigger and stronger. And the tentacles go out further. I've now done 12 consecutive launches that have done at least $1 million in sales. Half of those have done more than $3 million in sales. One of the things that I did, which I tried to keep quiet, was I actually sometimes run launches for other people. For Bill was one example. I totally suckered Bill in, or maybe he suckered me in. But I was like, I think you did. But still, you've got to admit the sales pitch is good. He really wanted to run a launch for him. And when I'm looking to run a launch, I'm as much interested in what strategic benefit does it have to me, in addition to any money I could make. Because I'm a big believer in thinking long term. And I think that's one of my competitive advantages, is I always think very long term. I'm like, I don't want to do a launch just to make some money from some guy. If it makes strategic sense, yes. And so working with Glaser Kennedy, did that make strategic sense? Yes. To be able to say that I basically consulted with them, or they were one of my clients, huge benefits. So Bill wanted me to run this launch. So I said, hey Bill, tell me what. If you were to take this product, it was Bill's copywriting course, awesome course, online course. If you were going to roll this thing out on your own, what kind of sales would you do? Just if you're going to mail out to your list, the way you would do a normal promotion of your list. You'd say $100,000. I'm like, cool. And that's awesome. I mean, wouldn't it be nice to just be able to send an email out and make $100,000 from a product? I mean, that's a power, power, power list. So I took him at face value, and Bill's like a very straight up guy. And I said, OK, how about this? We'll do a launch. We'll use your list. We'll try to bring in some partners. And what we'll do is, first $100,000 is yours. And once we get behind $100,000, you give me X%. I think it was 10%. I'm not that cheap anymore. But I think it was 10%. Because I did. I wanted the feather in my cap. And so, I mean, how can you pass up on that? He gets all the money he would normally make. Plus, he gets 90% of everything on the upside. And so they said yes. And we did it. And I think the sales were somewhere between $700,000 and $750,000. So it worked out. It worked out. It was OK. So I've got a question for you. See, I even wrote questions to remind me to ask you this question. At this point, how many of you would be interested in me running a launch for you? Can you raise your hands up high? OK. That's the typical reaction. A lot of people are interested in me running launches. The problem is, I got no interest in doing it. I don't want to run your launches. I want to focus on my business. I care about my business a lot more than I care about your business. I'm sorry. All your product launch formulas are out there. I love my product launch formula owners. You guys are awesome. You're my favorite people. Absolutely. But in reality, if I'm going to put in the work to do a launch, I want to get all the benefit. Because as you saw with Andrew, the benefit isn't just the dollars. I might do a launch for someone and only get, maybe I get 15% or 20%. And some of these launches I've done over $3 million. So 15% of $3 million, a lot of money, $450,000. Not too many consultants are getting paid $450,000, especially for working for three or four weeks. But the benefits to that launch go so far beyond that. There's the list built. There's the positioning. There's all the new buyers that you can sell additional stuff to. There's all the momentum. It's just a lot of reciprocal benefits, very, very powerful. And I'd rather have all those benefits in my business. So if I'm going to work on a launch, I'd rather have it be my launch. So lots and lots and lots of people wanted to hire me. In fact, my brother, who's in my business, he's a partner in portion of it. He works for me in a portion of it. And for a while, it was almost his full-time job just to say no to people. I'm just like a nice guy. I have a hard time saying no. So I'm actually defenseless here. You usually want to go to events. I bring my brother just because he makes people feel so good when he says no. He's a nice guy too, but he's much better at saying no than I am. And so that was his full-time job, literally. Because I'd have very influential people that I wanted to maintain my relationship with. So I didn't want to just blow them off. So I sent them to John, and John blew them off in a very nice and elegant fashion. So anyways, lots and lots of people wanted to hire me. These clients had paid me a ton of money, but I was over it. They were happy to pay me tons of money. But I just could not handle the deal flow. It's just crazy. I guarantee you, right now I've told you, well, let me be explicit. I don't do this. I do not run launches for anyone. I do not do it. But I guarantee you, after I get off the stage, like 60, you got to come up and ask me, can you run my launch? It happens every time I get on stage. And so I'm like, OK, I got this massive deal flow. And there's clearly value. I just don't want to do it. I don't want to work on other people's stuff. So that led me to two questions. How can I attract and train and groom some talent, some people that can do what I do, that can help me with these client launches? And I wasn't sure if they would be employees, or if they would be partners, or if there would be an agency type. I had no idea. I just asked this question, how can I get some talent to do this? And then how can I make money while I'm doing that? So those are the questions I started with. And I went down a long road. And I asked a lot of people their opinion. I know I talked to Evan Pagan for a long time. I'm pretty sure I talked to Bill for a long time about that. I asked a lot of people. I asked my mastermind groups. And I went through a bunch of different ideas and iterations. Talked to Jay Abraham. One of the nice things is I tend to be, for some people, I don't like to run their launches. But I'm OK with helping them with their launches. Like, I'm OK taking a phone call. Or if they go into a pre-launch, like Evan Pagan. I don't know if you guys know him, but he's a brilliant, brilliant marketer. He's doing somewhere between $20 and $30 million a year selling in a variety of markets, completely online. And he's typical. Like, when he wanted that launch about, I don't know, four or five weeks ago, he gets a call from me. And it's like, Evan, how can I help you? I watched that first video. And I think, I like this. I didn't like this. You got to change this. Do this. So a lot of people tend to get calls from me like that. So one of them was Jay Abraham. And I gave him some help. And what's really nice is that it ends up always coming back to you. What I'm about to show you, he gave me some really great help. And it didn't cost me $7,000 an hour. So the solution was what I call product launch manager. And that's really what I'm here to talk about. This is basically, I put together a full out end to end program to teach people how to be a consultant and run these launches. OK, so now, like I said earlier, I'm a very much a long term strategic thinker. So I want to walk you through this, because I think this is where a lot of the real value is. So what did I want from this program? I wanted high quality people. I wanted to enhance my brand. This brand's worth a lot of money to me. I didn't want to screw that up. Very, very important to me. I wanted to maintain the perceived value of product launch formula. Product launch formula costs $2,000. So if I was going to train people a super set of this, they would have to know product launch formula plus a whole other piece of how to manage attract, manage clients. This is a bigger piece. So product launch formula is $2,000. This had to be at least, it had to be more than that. And it had to be significantly more. It had to be at least $4,000. So I'm like, OK, this has to be a $4,000 program. But I don't like that price. So I said, we're going to make it $5,000. Because I figured if you can get $4,000 from someone, you can get $5,000. Is that? I mean, OK. So I decided it's got to be $5,000. Am I going to take a cut of their business? I don't know. Couldn't figure that one out. That was hard to figure out. I just walked by that question. I was going to have to make a lot of money for it to interest me. And if I was going to sell for $5,000, and I'm an online guy, I am not shipping. There's been times where I've shipped CDs and books. But I didn't want to do that. I wanted to do it completely online, deliver as an online program. And so how do you sell a bunch of videos? I mean, you can go to YouTube and get a lot of videos for free. How do you sell a bunch of videos for $5,000? I'm going to need some real firepower to do this. And then I looked at my assets. This is a process that I always go through. It's like, what are my assets here? So I had a strong personal reputation. Product Launch Formula had a great reputation as a product. I had these crazy successes with Product Launch Formula that had showed the market over the years. I had Product Launch Formula that's established on a $2,000 product. I had some great joint venture partners that I knew would promote for me, because they make a fortune when they promote for me. I knew I had hyperresponses on my list. And this is something I go to a lot. Every one of you has hyperresponses on your list that I really love what you do. I mean, all of us are hyperresponses for Dan. Otherwise, we wouldn't be here, right? We are on his hyperresponsive list. Every list has got those. And I often like, how can I leverage those hyperresponses? Because beyond just money, there are other ways. And you're going to see how I did that. Also, I run very good events. I'm good at teaching from the stage. And there was huge pent-up demand for me to run people's launches. So I dumped all these into the mix. This is all part of the magic sauce. OK, the dangers I identified. No one had ever sold an online course like this at $5,000. I put four, because that was my minimum, but I knew I wanted five. I was inventing a profession, good and bad to that. There's no proven demand for this product launch manager. I just had an inkling that was out there. I was putting my reputation with my partners online. This is a big one, because my partners love me because I make them a ton of money. When they promote for me, they know my product converts. They make a ton of money. But if I come out and roll out something and it doesn't make much money, then I've screwed the pooch. That's bad. So you're only as good as your last hit. And so I didn't want to. It's like $5,000. That's a risk. But it's one thing to run that risk to your list and screw up. It's another thing to run it to your JV's list and screw up. That's bad. You send a few emails out to your list, put together a promotion for your list. You mess up, oh, bummer, I really wanted to make that money, but I didn't just destroy my brand. Just throw my reputation. Partners, completely different. It's a highway act. Potential loss of control of the brand. I grab a lot of people all of a sudden now out there saying they were Jeff Walker's this and Jeff Walker that. I told them they couldn't say they were Jeff Walker certified because of the next line at legal stuff. Where are you guys? Dixon, Nanton? Yeah, these guys told me I shouldn't call it certified. So they put the fear of God in me. And I'm like, OK, no certified. But then I still had these people running around saying they were certified. So I had to chase them down. So there's potential loss of control of the brand. There was the legal stuff, which thankfully that conversation was 18 months ago, and I don't ever have it again, because I'm just not a big fan of legal stuff. And it was a fundamentally different product and different market. I was selling to business owners about how to launch their product. Now I'm selling to people, like here, drop your profession and go into starting a new profession that I just invented and pay me $5,000 for it. So those are some of the dangers I identified. So my strategy was I was going to two-step it. I've just always loved the concept of two-step right from those little classified ads to get the free report. So I did my version of the two-step here. And what I did was I started with what we call an internal launch. So that was the launch to my list. And like I said, it's always a lot less risky to run it to your list. I started with a very high-end event, because, well, I think it's a lot easier to sell a very high-end event than it is to sell a very high-priced series of online videos to download. So I started with a really high-end event. I followed that with a joint venture launch of the home study version. It was the one for the show, two for the dough. So I also love keeping people guessing about what my motives are. So what I did was, and this is the part of the event, so I don't know what's on the next slide here. OK, here we go. Step one. So if I'm going to sell a $5,000 series of videos online, like I said, I needed firepower. So I loved the idea of setting up the apples to oranges comparison. I sold a live event. It's a $25,000 event. I figured if I'm going to end up selling a $5,000 product, if I say, well, this is the home study version of A, that is much better if it was $25,000. And I knew I had those hyperresponses on my list. So those hyperresponses just love to buy, and they love to pay high prices. So I was going to leverage those hyperresponses. I would also have got our, it was important for me to create some successes out of this group of people. So I wanted to work with them closely. So this live event, we limited to 50 primary spots. They could bring a partner or whatever, but it's 50 spots. And we put together a sequence for them. I'll show you a little bit of the sequence and some of the triggers we hit during the sequence. And it's sort of fun watching Dan go through this stuff, because I was like, holy cow, I did some of that stuff. So how do you sell a $25,000 program? You sell it, I sold it by selling it's $5,000 down and $20,000 out of your profits. These people are in these seats. Part of their sales pitch when they go out into the world is they were Jeff Walker trained. They were at his ultra exclusive $25,000 event. So they love the idea of going to a $25,000 event, even though they only had to pay $5,000. And I was betting on the come for everything else. We also brought in launch sheets. These are people that wanted launches brand. We had brought two of them, and they spent like $25,000, $30,000 plus a percent of their launch to come in. And we'd spend the day planning out their launch. And we'd split the people into teams and go off. And they would invent their launch and come back. It was a very interactive, really killer event. 50 people in there, with the guests and stuff, 80 people. Small room, five days, very intense, long days, and a real training. Of course, I was running the full AV for the whole entire thing, right? We had everything getting videotaped, the breakout sessions, everything getting videotaped, everything captured. All the work we did for the two launch sheets, all being captured. That became the home study program. OK. Let me just make sure I got. So we had extremely, because I wanted these people to be very, very successful, we had a very rigorous selection process. We literally had, for those 50 spots, I think we had 130 people apply. And my brother, the Hatchet man, he actually called, I mean, we ran him through a full application. And this doesn't make them feel like they're scarcity. This is like a real application. And John ended up calling, 70 or 80 of them. And we let 50 of them in. But we really cherry picked. This was going back to your assets. What are your assets? And I had those hyper responses. And you know, you do have that top 1% on your list and that top 4% on your list. And those are the people we wanted in the seats. Because we were going to use them as proof going forward to sell the home study program. So we used this live event, the position of the $25,000 We used having awesome people in the room. A lot of them were leaders. A lot of them were influencers. The social media was going nuts during that event. They were landing clients during the event based on Twitter. People were tweeting about wanting to hire one of these people. That's gold going forward, right? I also created a platinum level group. Now a lot of people say, how much of that money did you actually collect out of the back end at extra $20,000? What we did was we said, when we got them in the room, we said, hey, I spent five days teaching you. This is awesome. You got so much knowledge. But the reality is Monday is going to come. You're going to be flying home. And once you get out of this environment, it's going to be a lot harder to keep going. So you really want to join this coaching program. And the cool thing about this coaching program is it's not actually going to cost you any money at all. It's actually going to save you $10,000. Because if you just pay me $10,000 to be in my coaching program, because I'm so interested in you being in this program, I'll waive the $20,000 you're going to owe me out of profits. So of the 50 people in the room, I think it was like 37 took it. And the other, I don't know, it might have been, it was insane. It was almost, let's say it's between 35 and 45, closer to 45, I think. The only ones that didn't were the people that can't wear employees. They were sent by their company to the training. And the people that sent them that training, they regretted it. Because the first day I spent an entire day telling them, you guys should be getting, don't be working for 11, get 10 or 15% of these launches. So I don't think it worked out too well for those companies. OK, so step two, I took the social proof in a $25,000 event, which was a rapid sell out social proof. I mean, social media, the buzz was there. We had great, quick student success, because we hand picked these people to have success. And we did this home study course within three months. Basically, we used a video. We added a bunch of supplemental material, but we had that video, so that really gives a head start on the product. And of course, I didn't sell us a $5,000 program. Screw that. I sold us a $10,000 program. Same thing. $5,000 down, $5,000 out of profits. OK, so now I want to think what we got coming up. Pure genius with a J. This is trademark Frank Kern. So I didn't come up with that. That's supposed to be clever. It's funnier when Frank says it. And if we have time at the end, they ask me about Texas Holden because I've got a theory about that. So one of the big ideas, I've tried to build in some, translate into some of Dan's terms. One of the big ideas we came out with was this is a real business, making money as an internet marketing consultant. So there's a lot of pure schlock being sold online to IM people that really love this internet marketing stuff, and they're going from bounce it from one thing to another. This is a real business. And in my opinion, it really was because the man truly was there, and there's a ton of money we made running people's launches. The hook, one of the hooks was pretty simple. We had a bunch of hooks. We had this just laden with hooks. One of the hooks is you can make a ton of money, you don't need a list, you don't need a product. So there are so many people online there, these internet marketing junkies, that just study this stuff and hang out in forums and read and read and read and watch videos. And they love this internet marketing stuff, but they've never started. They never started building a list, which is just absolutely insane. I mean, it takes five minutes to start building a list now with Facebook. I mean, give me a break. Or they don't have a product. So you can make a ton of money, no list, no product. That was one of the hooks. So I started off this with, and actually, I'm going to mix up, I'm going to give you some of the stuff we did. So I did two launch sequences, that internal launch and the external launch, the JV launch. The first one for the $25,000 event, the second one for the $10,000 product. And I'm going to actually mix, because I didn't want to walk through two sequences. So some of this stuff is mixed in. And it's interesting when I look back how I shifted stuff. So I started off the internal launch with what I called the tennis court video. Now, a lot of these online launches, we use video these days. So we'll create an 18 minute, a 20 minute video. The first one is usually about the opportunity. So I personally, I don't have the fanciest lifestyle in the world. I've got some really nice bicycles. I've got some really nice guitars. But I drive a Ford F-350. I'm not the Bentley guy. And I think part of my shtick is the Ashuk's thing. And I tend to be pretty believable. But I'm just not the standing in front of the Bentley guy. But as Dan talked about, which one was it? It was the hype. And it was the larger than life. It's a very useful thing. I'm quite aware of that. So I go to my assets again. Not the Bentley, not the Ferrari, because I live in Colorado in a little tiny town way out in the mountains, 300 miles from Denver. And you've got to drive up a dirt road for a mile and a half to get to my house. The 911 doesn't work so well on the dirt road. Hence the F-350. But I do live in this beautiful spot with 350 acres of woods around me. And I've got my own tennis court. So the tennis court video. And so the first video was basically me walking through the woods talking about how I am not about ferrari's and gold-plated helicopters. But I do like to play tennis on Friday afternoons with my buddies. So I put in the tennis court. Oh, by the way, the tennis court, we actually, this is where we keep our rackets. And this little shelf right here, that's where we put our margarita blender on Friday afternoons. Because of course, I don't have to work on Friday afternoons. I take the whole afternoon off and I go hang out in the woods. That wasn't said. It's obviously implied. But then I started talking about, I have a lifestyle business. I have people that pay me a ton of money to do these launches. But because my business is all about lifestyle, I don't like to run them anymore. And I can't keep up with the demand. So I need someone to run these launches for me. So that's the reason why. There's massive reason why in this tennis court video. I also went on to tell them, we're going to get back to the adventure. So this is a lifestyle. It was opportunity. And it was a reason why. All in that first video. I went on to tell them that one of the reasons I wanted to do this program was to attract some welled clients, some massive, massive clients. And I knew if I had these trained product launch managers, they would be out in the world. And eventually they would bring me some massive clients that I would run the launch and split the profits with them. So there's lots and lots of reason why I was willing to pass up these opportunities to run these launches. I also told them my numbers. At that point, my two prior launches had made like a quarter million each from the launches. So of course, I shared those numbers. And I said, not all the launches are like that. You might only make $10,000 on a launch. But it's not bad money. So then flashing forward, that was for launching to my list. Flashing forward, launching to my joint venture partners lists. That one, I did leave with hype. The first word taught me on my mouth. And I'm out in the mountains again, trees and stuff. What if you could make a ton of money and you didn't need to have a list, a product, or any customer service or anything like that? Those were literally the first words out of my mouth. From there, we went into describing the opportunity, then the building of the trust, then building the legend of Jeff there. But with my list, I started off first with me and lifestyle and reason why. So it's a little bit of a switch for the markets, for the world that didn't know me, hype right off to hook them instantly. One of the things we did, and so we built, we stepped so much stuff into this whole process. It was just insane as I was going through these videos. So I'm only going to be able to show you some of this, and I'm going to bounce around a little bit. One of the concepts we used, I put out what I called the fish video. And this, it goes after that famous saying, teach a man to fish, and he'll ask for a fish sandwich. We've all heard that one. Or maybe you heard the incorrect one. What's the incorrect one? That's a give a man a fish and leave for the day, teach an official, and he'll leave for the rest of his life. But the reality is, is people don't want to learn how to fish. They certainly don't want to clean the fish. And they'd really rather not cook the fish. And so we basically played with this idea. It's reality. And we played with that reality and taught that. That was a piece of pre-launch content that we're teaching. And basically, of course, the punchline is, this is why people want to hire you to do a launch. They don't want to do the launch themselves. They bought my product. The best prospects in the world, of course, are the people that bought my product. And they don't want to do it. They learn how to do it. They know the value of it, but they don't want to do it. They want you to do it for them. So we played quite a bit with the fish video. One of the other things we played with quite a bit was, so there is this whole subculture of people that love internet marketing. They just love the stuff. They know everything about it. They haven't done anything, but they know everything about it. And their neighbors think they're like wizards, because they start talking about this. In fact, they can't even talk to anyone. Everyone, their eyes glaze over as soon as they start talking about squeeze pages and paper click ads and stuff. They just, their spouse goes to sleep. Their brothers and sisters, parents, they don't care about that stuff. It's like, oh yeah, he's talking about that computer crap. I don't care. But I knew that was out there. So one of the things I talked about, one of the, one person who I helped a little bit with a launch was Tony Robbins. And I had this, was meeting with him. And it wasn't just me. It was Frank Kern was there, and Mike Caneggs, and Mike Phil same, I think. And so we met with him. And I said, OK, so Tony, what you need to do is you just need to put together a squeeze page. How many people don't know what a squeeze page is? Pretty much everyone knows. It's just a page where you get opt-ins. So I'm like, Tony, you need to do this, this, and put the squeeze page, put this up there. And Tony literally stops me. He's like, Jeff, what's a squeeze page? And I was like, sort of flabbergasted. I'm part of that culture. I assume everyone else has squeeze pages. OK, Tony's running big companies, makes a lot of money, knows what he's doing, doesn't know squeeze pages. So I explained, gave him the 30 second version of a squeeze page. So that was one of the things I talked about in my videos, was you guys, you have the specialized knowledge that the rest of the world doesn't have. I also told the story of me going to a mastermind with some real business people, not like my kind of business, but like making ball bearings and stuff like that. And how all those people, they just wanted my business. As soon as they heard about my business, they really wanted it. And I was like Dan said, there's always that grass is always greener. But I know I didn't want the ball bearing business. I was pretty good with my business. So I basically told the story of Tony and the story of these real business owners wanting what we have. The amazing business that we have that allows us to have this internet lifestyle. They want it. And they not only want it, but they're coming to get it. They're learning about us. They're coming to get it from us. They want this knowledge. And we have this first starter advantage on them. In the land of the blind, the one-eyed man is king. I hit that really hard. Because if someone's going to buy this, they have to buy into the fact that they have the knowledge to be able to actually do it. Because when they're buying it, they're actually thinking about doing it. Even if they're not going to end up doing it, they think they're going to do it. Or at least they're telling themselves they're going to do it, right? So if they're going to tell themselves they're going to do something, then they have to have the faith that they can actually do it. So a lot of this pre-launch was instilling this idea that they had this secret knowledge. Just by watching my video, they had enough knowledge about internet marketing that would blow away most people. So that was a big theme as we went through the pre-launch. We know something in there after it. Marketing gods. This is something that I just sort of came up with on the fly when I was on the podium at that $25,000 event. And it turned out to be inspired. I mean, it was just like at some point in there, I was trying to convince them how awesome they were because they were awesome. We put them through the selection process, and they paid a fortune to be there. So I was trying to, as clear, I had to instill in them that they were awesome if they were going to go out and have success. And them going out and having success, that small select group having some real success there, was important to me to build my product down the road. And somehow down there, I just stopped. I'm like, you are marketing gods. There are tons and tons of content creators, very few marketing gods. You're the marketing gods. And I'm sorry if I'm offending anyone here, but it just did come out spur of the moment. And they grabbed onto that, and they started tweeting that, and they put it on Facebook that they were officially marketing gods because I told them they were marketing gods. And this became a theme going forward. That basically I could just instill this marketing godhood in these folks. And so down the road, when I go to buy the thing, who doesn't want to spend $5,000 to become a god? And this is actually, I took that clip from the script. Most of these videos do tend to be scripted. And so I actually pulled it from the script. If you can speak internet marketing, you are in a separate class from the rest of the people walking the face of the earth. It worked. I don't know. You guys are going to have a hard time reading some of these slides. What I actually, I apologize for the world's ugliest. I'm not like a big design guy. And what I did was I cut some of the slides from my videos, and I dropped them straight in here. So you're probably not going to be able to read these things. But this is a slide from one of my pre-launched videos. And it just says, the launch asset discovery process. A key tool in your arsenal in your client will think you walk on water. Again, hammering the idea that they're going to have the authority once they go to this training. They won't know you're just following the product launch manager script. The initial timing discussion. Easy for you, but a big aha moment for your client. Planning the offer. Your client will think they have this one nailed down, but you'll stump them in the first three minutes. You will see this pattern over and over. Even among your biggest and most prestigious clients, you'll be able to identify major gaps in their strategy and tactics simply by following your basic product launch manager training. So again, just instilling the idea that you can do it. That you have the secret sauce already built in you, and now we're going to just amp it up with this training. You, a knight in shining armor. I think that was the status one, number 15 here. Creating proof. I'm a big one on proof. And this actually, I did this launch in late 2009. So this is before the new FTC regulations. And one of the reasons I did it in late 2009 was because in early 2009, I was reading Dan Kennedy's newsletter, and it said these changes are coming. And I'm like, OK, I'm going to need some proof for this thing. So I, and I'm going to need to create some proof and use the proof, so I better do it before the new regulations. So I surveyed my list. I love surveys. Key component of product launch for me. I surveyed my list. And big surprise, they all wanted to hire me, right? But I've done a lot of work with surveys, and I know how to structure them. So I asked them this question, do you want to hire Jeff Walker to run your launch? And of course they all said yes. And then I followed it up. Next question was, and they couldn't see the next question, pop, next question. If you can't hire me, would you like to hire one of my product launch managers? It's one of my trained product launch managers. Of course the answer is, well, yeah. Even though such a thing did not exist yet, right? This is before the training. Well, sure enough, 74.2% said they wanted to hire one of my product launch managers, one of my trained product launch managers. And that picture is just literally a picture from the survey software. So then this is a slide from my video. It's like, hey folks, the demand's there. I sent this out to my list, and I gave them all the numbers. I sent it to my list of 2,300 people, and of the 2,300 people, 312 people said yes. And of those, 74% said yes to Jeff, and 74% yes to one of my managers. Bingo, there you are, 228 clients just waiting for you. I've only got 50 spots. Who wants them? Right? And then I played further games with the math. And I really wasn't playing any real games at all, but I mailed that first email out to the survey to 2,000 people. That's 2,000 of my hyper-responsives, right? It wasn't my whole list of 70 or 80,000 people. Then I said, what if I sent that email out to my, I asked them that. You know I've got 70,000 other people. I only sent this to 2,000. Is there a demand? Yes, there's a demand. Do you want one of those 50 spots? My $800,000 bet, I went into that. OK, 50 of you, you're spending $5,000. I'm only asking for $5,000 down. It's a $25,000 program. That means I'm taking a $20,000 bet on each one of you. I'm taking an $800,000 gamble that I can actually teach this and make you into marketing gods. So in other words, there's a little bit more playing with the math. Then coming out of that event, I obviously had some great successes because we handpicked these people. Jonathan Drake, no list, no product, no launch experience. $18,000 payday as a product launch manager. He did it in just 10 days, literally the first 10 days after he left the program. Brandon Fredrickson, more than $500,000 in debt due to medical bills. One of the objections I knew I'd get is what if I live in Malaysia, or what if I live in Russia, or what if I live in Northern California, whatever. There's always that objection. Well, I showed him Brandon. Geography doesn't matter because he was actually quarantined in a hospital room when he ran that launch. 100% true. The guy had a staff infection, ran the launch from the hotel room. Can't make that stuff up. More proof. Just piling on proof. This is where I basically, this is between the first launch and the second launch. I basically went and I created a directory that I would list my product launch managers in. And then I emailed my big list and I said, hey, do you want to have access to my product launch managers? Go here. And bingo, we had 1,100 people sign up literally overnight for this list. So those are just some of the things we built in. We stacked one thing after another. And we got a lot of them on this list. It's basically you've got the gold right here. And it's just thinking about how can you take your assets and build them into a process where you're leveraging as many of these as possible. So our results, the initial event brought, we sold that thing out. It brought in about $300,000. The home study, we rolled that thing out. We sold 440 of them at $5,000. We also had a six pay at $1,000 a month. So we did about $2.5 million on that. Created a coaching group. I did generate client income. In other words, I had product launch managers running launches, and we were getting a portion of that. And we extended our brand. Now we did have some messiness. One of the last thing I'll leave you with this wasn't all beauty and grace, because I had complete control over my brand prior to this. And after it, now I had hundreds of people running around telling the world they were Jeff Walker this and Jeff Walker that. And it did create a little bit of messiness for the brand. We did have a pretty tight relationship with them, so we were able to manage it fairly well. But there was definitely some messiness there. So I think that's all I got.
